---
title: "The Activation-Control NTK Duality, From First Principles"
subtitle: "What it means for a log-gate controller to imitate weight-space SGD, derived step by step"
date: 2026-06-12
abstract: |
  The central theoretical claim behind signed log-gate controllers is the
  *activation-control NTK duality*: a small set of bounded multiplicative
  gates on hidden channels can locally reproduce, on a chosen observable, the
  effect of a full-weight stochastic-gradient step on a frozen network. This
  report derives that claim and its test from first principles. It builds the
  neural-tangent-kernel picture of one SGD step from a Taylor expansion;
  explains the "mirror" coordinates in which gates live and compose; states
  the duality as a precise range condition and gives three equivalent readings
  of the word "dual"; derives the metric ridge-projected solver and every
  residual it reports, including their error terms; and proves why a failed
  secant test does not refute duality. Two worked examples — a one-block
  linear model and a two-layer MLP — are developed in complete, self-contained
  detail, with every quantity (the tangent, the kernel, the range condition,
  the exact realizing update, the second-order realization error, the secant
  error) computed by hand; the MLP example yields a structural result about
  *which* weight updates a gate site can absorb exactly. Five appendix
  tutorials make the document self-contained: the calculus toolkit
  (Jacobians, JVP/VJP, Taylor remainders), the neural tangent kernel, mirror
  descent and exponentiated gradients, least squares with ridge and
  Lagrangian duality, and conjugate gradients.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Purpose and how to read this {#sec-purpose}

Two companion documents describe the method around this theory: the
[method specification](scientific_method.qmd) gives the full
framework-independent recipe, and the
[technical report](ntkmirror_technical_report.qmd) maps it onto the
implementation. Both *state* the duality test; neither derives it. This
report is the derivation. It assumes calculus, linear algebra, and basic
familiarity with training neural networks by gradient descent — and nothing
else: every other prerequisite (neural tangent kernels, mirror descent,
ridge regression and Lagrangian duality, conjugate gradients, the
forward/reverse-mode derivative toolkit) is taught in a short self-contained
tutorial in the appendices (@sec-app-calculus through @sec-app-cg). If a
concept feels unfamiliar at any point, the intended reading order is: jump
to the matching appendix, then return.

The logical spine is short:

1. One SGD step moves a frozen network's outputs, to first order, along a
   computable direction — the *NTK displacement* (@sec-ntk).
2. A log-gate controller is a tiny coordinate system ("chart") whose motions
   also move the outputs, along the columns of a Jacobian $B$ (@sec-chart).
3. **Duality** is the claim that direction (1) lies in the span of
   directions (2) — a range condition, testable by a least-squares
   projection whose residual is the verdict (@sec-duality, @sec-solver).
4. Every reported diagnostic is an error term of that test, each with a
   derivable meaning (@sec-residuals, @sec-secant).
5. Two models small enough to solve by hand — a one-block linear model
   (@sec-worked-linear) and a two-layer MLP (@sec-worked-mlp) — make every
   abstraction concrete. The worked examples are written to be read on
   their own: they deliberately restate, in place, every definition and
   formula they use, at the cost of repetition.

# Glossary {#sec-glossary}

Terms of art, each defined once here and used consistently after.

| Term | Meaning | Where derived |
|---|---|---|
| frozen model | the trained network $f_\theta$; its weights $\theta$ are never permanently changed | — |
| gate, controller | a bounded scalar $s_j$ multiplying one hidden channel by $e^{s_j}$; a controller is a sparse set of $k$ such gates | @sec-chart |
| mirror coordinates | the additive log-space coordinates $s$ in which multiplicative channel scales become linear; the "mirror" in the method's name | @sec-mirror, @sec-app-mirror |
| support batch $S$ | the training examples whose loss gradient defines the weight step being imitated (terminology from few-shot learning: the data that *supports* the adaptation) | @sec-ntk |
| calibration batch $C$ | the examples on which the *effect* of any intervention is observed; the measurement apparatus, not the training signal | @sec-ntk |
| observable / projection $P$ | a fixed finite list of $D$ logit coordinates on the calibration batch; all comparisons happen in $\mathbb{R}^D$ | @sec-observables |
| target field $d$ | the measured displacement of the observable caused by one small weight-SGD step; "field" because it is defined at every controller state, like a vector field on the chart | @sec-ntk |
| gate tangent $B$ | the $D\times k$ Jacobian of the observable with respect to the gate values, at the current state | @sec-chart |
| chart | a smooth parameterization of a family of model behaviors; the gates are a $k$-dimensional chart, the weights a $p$-dimensional one | @sec-duality |
| range condition / duality | the claim $d \in \operatorname{range}(B)$ (approximately, with a bounded realizing update) | @sec-duality |
| range residual | relative distance from $d$ to $\operatorname{range}(B)$, as estimated by the ridge solve — the duality verdict | @sec-residuals |
| realized residual | relative error after actually *taking* the solved gate step (tests chart curvature, not range) | @sec-residuals |
| secant diagnostic | a test of whether the *trained* controller is explained by its tangent at initialization; expressly **not** a duality test | @sec-secant |
| empirical NTK | the kernel $K = J J^{\top}$ built from output Jacobians, governing first-order output motion under gradient steps | @sec-ntk, @sec-app-ntk |
| metric $M$ | the inner product in which gate motion is measured; the activation metric makes "small $u$" mean "small induced activation change" | @sec-metric |

# Part I — What one SGD step does to a model's outputs {#sec-ntk}

## Setup

Let $f_\theta$ be the frozen network with weights $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p$.
Fix two batches:

- the **support batch** $S$: examples with supervised completions, defining
  a scalar training loss $L_S(\theta)$ (mean next-token cross-entropy over
  supervised positions). This is the data an SGD step would learn from.
- the **calibration batch** $C$: examples on which we *watch* what any
  intervention does. Nothing is ever trained on $C$; it is the instrument.

Let $z_S(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{D_S}$ collect the model's logit coordinates
at the support batch's supervised positions, and
$\Phi(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ collect a fixed list of $D$ logit
coordinates on the calibration batch (the *observable*; its precise
construction is @sec-observables). Write the two Jacobians

$$
J_S \;=\; \frac{\partial z_S}{\partial \theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{D_S\times p},
\qquad
J_C \;=\; \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times p}.
$$

(Jacobians, directional derivatives, and the Taylor expansions used below
are reviewed in @sec-app-calculus.)

## One SGD step, expanded to first order

The support loss depends on $\theta$ only through the support logits,
$L_S(\theta) = \ell(z_S(\theta))$, so by the chain rule the weight gradient
factors:

$$
g_\theta \;=\; \nabla_\theta L_S \;=\; J_S^{\top} g,
\qquad
g \;=\; \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial z_S} \in \mathbb{R}^{D_S}.
\tag{1}
$$

($g$ is the *output-space* error signal; for cross-entropy it is
"predicted probabilities minus one-hot targets" at each supervised
position.) One SGD step with learning rate $\eta$ is
$\delta\theta = -\eta\, g_\theta = -\eta\, J_S^{\top} g$. Taylor-expand the
observable around $\theta$:

$$
\Phi(\theta + \delta\theta) - \Phi(\theta)
\;=\; J_C\,\delta\theta + O(\lVert\delta\theta\rVert^2)
\;=\; \underbrace{-\,\eta\; J_C\, J_S^{\top}\, g}_{\textstyle d}
\;+\; O(\eta^2).
\tag{2}
$$

Equation (2) is the whole of the "NTK" content needed here. The matrix

$$
K_{CS} \;=\; J_C\, J_S^{\top} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{D\times D_S}
\tag{3}
$$

is the **empirical neural tangent kernel** block between calibration and
support coordinates (Jacot, Gabriel & Hongler 2018; Lee et al. 2019):
entry $(a,b)$ is the inner product of the weight-gradients of observable
coordinate $a$ and support coordinate $b$. Its meaning is mechanical:
*training on support coordinate $b$ moves observable coordinate $a$ in
proportion to how aligned their weight-sensitivities are.* To first order in
$\eta$, gradient descent on the weights is kernel gradient descent on the
outputs with kernel $K$; networks operating in this first-order regime are
called *lazy* (Chizat, Oyallon & Bach 2019). A standalone tutorial on the
NTK, including the gradient-flow derivation and the relation to
infinite-width theory, is @sec-app-ntk. We call

$$
d \;=\; -\,\eta\, K_{CS}\, g
\tag{4}
$$

the **target field**: the function-space velocity of weight-SGD, projected
onto the observable. "Field" because $d$ is defined at every controller
state (the Jacobians in the kernel formula (3) are evaluated with the
controller attached at its current state, so $d$ changes as the controller
moves) — it assigns a displacement vector to each point of the gate chart,
exactly like a vector field.

## Why $d$ is measured, not computed from the formula {#sec-fd}

The kernel displacement formula (4) is a first-order prediction. The method
does not trust it: it *performs* the step and measures the secant,

$$
\hat d \;=\; \Phi(\theta - \eta\, g_\theta) - \Phi(\theta),
\qquad \text{weights restored exactly afterwards.}
\tag{5}
$$

The error structure of (5) determines how to choose $\eta$. Expanding one
order further, with $H$ the second derivative of $\Phi$ along $g_\theta$:

$$
\hat d \;=\; -\eta\, J_C\, g_\theta
\;+\; \tfrac{\eta^2}{2}\, H[g_\theta, g_\theta] \;+\; O(\eta^3),
$$

so the **truncation error** of treating $\hat d$ as the linear response is
*relatively* of size $\tfrac{\eta}{2}\,\lVert H[g_\theta,g_\theta]\rVert /
\lVert J_C g_\theta\rVert$ — it grows linearly in $\eta$. Against it stands
the **noise floor**: $\Phi$ is computed in finite precision with absolute
error $\sim \varepsilon\lVert\Phi\rVert$, so the relative noise on $\hat d$
is $\sim 2\varepsilon \lVert\Phi\rVert / (\eta\lVert J_C g_\theta\rVert)$ —
it grows as $1/\eta$. The two cross at an intermediate optimum; in practice
one sweeps $\eta$ over a few decades and uses the plateau where
$\hat d/\eta$ is stable. This is the derivation behind the guidance "too
large measures a nonlinear weight move; too small drowns in noise; sweep
it." Reference value: $\eta = 10^{-5}$, with low-precision inference dtypes
pushing the noise floor up and demanding care.

# Part II — The controller as a chart, and its mirror coordinates {#sec-chart-part}

## Mirror coordinates: why gates live in log space {#sec-mirror}

The intervention multiplies selected hidden channels by positive scales
$m_j > 0$. Positive scales form a *multiplicative* group: applying scale
$m^{(1)}$ and then $m^{(2)}$ to the same channel yields $m^{(1)}m^{(2)}$.
The logarithm $s = \log m$ converts this group to an *additive* one:
composition of controllers becomes vector addition, scalar weighting of a
controller becomes scalar multiplication, and "no intervention" becomes the
origin. The gate values $s$ are therefore the natural *linear coordinates*
for a multiplicative intervention.

This is precisely the change of variables used by **mirror descent**
(Nemirovski & Yudin 1983; Beck & Teboulle 2003): for positive variables
under the negative-entropy mirror map
$\Psi(m)=\sum_c (m_c\log m_c - m_c)$, the dual ("mirror") coordinate is
$\nabla\Psi(m) = \log m$, and additive updates in the mirror coordinate are
exponentiated-gradient / multiplicative updates in the primal variable
(Kivinen & Warmuth 1997). A self-contained tutorial on mirror descent is
@sec-app-mirror. The signed log-gates *are* mirror coordinates of channel
scales; the box $|s_j|\le s_{\max}$ is a trust region imposed in mirror
space; and the composition rule "add the $s$ vectors, then clip" is
linearity in mirror coordinates. This is the **mirror** in the method's
name. (The name carries a second, informal sense — the controller *mirrors*
the weight-SGD field — which Part III makes precise.)

## The gate chart and its tangent {#sec-chart}

Fix the frozen weights and the $k$ selected (block, channel) gate sites.
The map

$$
F:\ \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^D,
\qquad
s \;\mapsto\; \text{observable of the gated model}
$$

is a smooth, $k$-parameter family of model behaviors as seen through the
observable — in differential-geometric language, a **chart**: a coordinate
system on a small patch of "behavior space". The weights provide another,
$p$-dimensional chart $\theta \mapsto \Phi(\theta)$ through the same
observable. The duality question of Part III is whether the small chart can
locally express a particular motion of the big one.

The chart's first derivative is the **gate tangent**

$$
B(s) \;=\; \frac{\partial F(s)}{\partial s} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{D\times k}.
\tag{6}
$$

Column $j$ has an explicit chain-rule form. Gate $j$ scales channel $c_j$ of
block $\ell_j$ at every token position $t$, so perturbing $s_j$ by
$\delta s_j$ perturbs that hidden coordinate by
$\delta h_{\ell_j,t,c_j} = e^{s_j} h_{\ell_j,t,c_j}\,\delta s_j$
(differentiate $e^{s}h$ in $s$), and propagates to the observable through
the network's downstream Jacobian:

$$
B_{:,j}(s)
\;=\;
\sum_{t}\;
\frac{\partial F}{\partial h_{\ell_j,t,c_j}}\;
e^{s_j}\, h_{\ell_j,t,c_j}(s).
\tag{7}
$$

Two facts in the tangent-column formula (7) drive everything later. First,
$B$ depends on the *data*: a channel that is silent on the calibration
batch ($h_{\ell_j,\cdot,c_j} = 0$) contributes a zero column — that gate
cannot move the observable at all, no matter what a weight update did to
it. Second, $B$ depends on $s$ itself, both through the explicit $e^{s_j}$
and because upstream gates change the hidden states feeding downstream
sites: the chart is *curved*, and its tangent must be re-evaluated wherever
it is used. Both facts become hand-computable in the worked examples
(@sec-worked-linear, @sec-worked-mlp).

## Observables, and a dimension-counting warning {#sec-observables}

All comparisons happen in a fixed projection of the calibration batch's
supervised next-token logits, built once from a baseline pass: per
supervised position, either the teacher-token coordinate (**target** mode,
$D = P$ for $P$ positions), the teacher plus the top-$k_{\mathrm{top}}$
baseline non-teacher tokens (**top-k** mode, $D = P(k_{\mathrm{top}}{+}1)$),
or the whole vocabulary (**full**, $D = PV$); logits are centered per
position so a uniform shift of all logits — which changes no probability —
is quotiented out. (Full construction in the
[method specification](scientific_method.qmd).)

The choice of $D$ is not cosmetic; it controls whether the duality test can
fail at all. $\operatorname{range}(B)$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^D$ of
dimension at most $\min(k, D)$:

- If $D \le k$ (e.g. **target** mode with a few hundred positions against
  $k = 5000$ gates), a generic $B$ has full row rank and its range is *all*
  of $\mathbb{R}^D$. The range condition then holds trivially, for any
  target whatsoever; the only informative diagnostics left are the size of
  the realizing update and the realization/box residuals.
- If $D \gg k$ (**top-k** or **full**), $\operatorname{range}(B)$ is a thin
  $k$-dimensional slice of a huge space and the range condition is a real,
  falsifiable claim.

This derives, rather than merely asserts, the documented failure mode
"projection too narrow: *target* may pass while *top-k*/*full* refutes."
A duality claim should always be made on an observable with $D$ comfortably
above $k$, or else accompanied by the update-norm and realization numbers.

# Part III — The duality claim {#sec-duality}

## Statement

> **Definition (local activation-control NTK duality).** Fix the frozen
> weights $\theta$, a controller state $s$, an observable $P$ with map $F$,
> a support batch $S$, and a step size $\eta$. Let $d$ be the measured
> target field (the displacement (5)) and $B = B(s)$ the gate tangent (the
> Jacobian (6)). The controller is **$\epsilon$-dual to weight-SGD** at
> this state and observable if there exists a gate update
> $u \in \mathbb{R}^k$ with
> $$
> \frac{\lVert d - B u\rVert}{\lVert d\rVert} \;\le\; \epsilon
> \qquad\text{and}\qquad
> s + u \in [-s_{\max}, s_{\max}]^k .
> $$

In words: *everything that one small weight-SGD step would do to the
observable, some bounded gate motion would also do, up to relative error
$\epsilon$.* The claim is local (it linearizes both charts at the current
point), observable-relative (it says nothing about coordinates outside
$P$), and data-relative (both $d$ and $B$ depend on the batches).

## Three precise readings of "dual" {#sec-readings}

The word is doing three jobs, all compatible.

**(a) Two charts on one behavior manifold.** Weights and gates are two
coordinate systems whose images overlap near the current behavior. Weight-SGD
defines a velocity through the big chart; duality says the small chart's
tangent space contains (the observable shadow of) that velocity. The gate
chart is then a faithful local "dual description" of this particular motion
— $k$ numbers standing in for $p$.

**(b) A Lagrangian dual variable.** The natural way to *find* the
realizing update is the smallest gate motion that does the job:

$$
\min_u\ \tfrac12\,\lVert u\rVert_M^2
\quad\text{s.t.}\quad B u = d ,
\tag{8}
$$

with $\lVert u\rVert_M^2 = u^{\top} M u$ for a positive diagonal metric $M$
(@sec-metric). Form the Lagrangian
$\mathcal{L}(u,\alpha) = \tfrac12 u^{\top}Mu - \alpha^{\top}(Bu - d)$ and set
$\partial_u \mathcal{L} = 0$:

$$
M u = B^{\top}\alpha
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
u = M^{-1}B^{\top}\alpha,
\qquad
\big(B M^{-1} B^{\top}\big)\,\alpha = d .
\tag{9}
$$

The solver of @sec-solver is literally this **dual system**: the unknown
$\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^D$ is the Lagrange multiplier of the constraint
"realize the field," and the gate update is reconstructed from it.
(Minimum-norm problems, Lagrange multipliers, and ridge regularization are
reviewed in @sec-app-ls.) Solving in the dual is also the only practical
choice: the dual system is $D\times D$ and accessed matrix-free, while the
primal normal equations would be $k\times k$.

**(c) Kernel imitation.** Define the **gate kernel**
$K^{\mathrm{gate}} = B M^{-1} B^{\top}$ on the observable, in exact analogy
with the weight NTK $K = J_C J_C^{\top}$. Ordinary gate training (gradient
descent of the support loss in $s$) moves the observable along
$-\,\eta_s\, B_C B_S^{\top} g$ — kernel descent with the *gate* kernel. The
duality claim is that the *weight*-kernel velocity (the target field (4))
lies in the range of the gate kernel; equivalently, gate-space dynamics can
imitate weight-space dynamics on this observable. This sharpens the
difference between the two training modes: standard fitting *uses* the gate
kernel to reduce the loss (and will happily move in directions weight-SGD
never would), while field-locked fitting (@sec-pathwise) *constrains* the
motion to imitate the weight kernel, and the residual reports where
imitation became impossible.

## What duality buys, and what it does not

If $\epsilon$-duality holds along a training path, then up to the
accumulated residuals, fine-tuning *as seen through this observable* could
have been performed by a $k$-parameter bounded, removable, composable
intervention instead of a $p$-parameter weight update — grounding the
practical claims (detachability, composition, memory) in the geometry of
the frozen network rather than in luck. What it does **not** claim: nothing
about coordinates outside the observable; nothing global in $s$ (the claim
is re-established locally at each state); nothing about *which* $u$ ordinary
loss-minimizing training will pick; and nothing when the box constraint is
the binding obstruction — which is why clipping has its own diagnostics.

# Part IV — Deriving the solver {#sec-solver}

## From the variational problem to the implemented system

The constrained minimum-norm problem (8) is brittle when $d$ has a
component outside $\operatorname{range}(B)$ — exactly the situation the
test must detect — so it is relaxed to a penalized form that is always
well-posed:

$$
u_\lambda \;=\; \arg\min_u\;
\lVert B u - d\rVert^2 \;+\; \lambda\,\lVert u\rVert_M^2 ,
\qquad \lambda > 0 .
\tag{10}
$$

Setting the gradient to zero: $B^{\top}(Bu - d) + \lambda M u = 0$, i.e.
$u_\lambda = (B^{\top}B + \lambda M)^{-1}B^{\top} d$ — a $k\times k$ system.
The **push-through identity** converts it to the $D\times D$ dual form.
From the algebraic identity

$$
B^{\top}\big(B M^{-1} B^{\top} + \lambda I\big)
\;=\;
\big(B^{\top}B + \lambda M\big)\, M^{-1} B^{\top},
$$

multiply on the left by $(B^{\top}B+\lambda M)^{-1}$ and on the right by
$(BM^{-1}B^{\top}+\lambda I)^{-1}$:

$$
u_\lambda
\;=\;
M^{-1} B^{\top}
\underbrace{\big(B M^{-1} B^{\top} + \lambda I\big)^{-1} d}_{\textstyle \alpha_\lambda}.
\tag{11}
$$

As $\lambda \to 0^+$, $u_\lambda$ converges to the minimum-$M$-norm
least-squares solution — the relaxation of (8) that projects $d$ onto
$\operatorname{range}(B)$ and realizes that projection with the smallest
gate motion (see @sec-app-ls for the standard theory). So the implemented
solve answers, in one shot, both halves of the duality question: *how much
of $d$ is reachable* (the residual) *and what is the cheapest way to reach
it* (the update).

## The activation metric as a pullback {#sec-metric}

What should "small $u$" mean? Euclidean length of $s$-perturbations is
arbitrary: a unit of $\delta s$ on a high-energy channel moves activations
far more than the same unit on a quiet channel. The principled choice is to
**pull back** the Euclidean metric on activations through the intervention:
since $\delta h_{c} = e^{s_c} h_{c}\,\delta s_c$,

$$
\lVert \delta h\rVert^2
= \sum_j \Big(\mathbb{E}\big[h_{\ell_j,\cdot,c_j}^2\big]\, e^{2 s_j}\Big)\,
\delta s_j^2
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
M_j = \mathbb{E}\big[h_{\ell_j,\cdot,c_j}^2\big]\, e^{2 s_j},
\tag{12}
$$

with the expectation over the calibration batch's positions at gate $j$'s
site. Under this $M$, the objective in the minimum-norm problem (8)
measures *induced activation displacement*, and the minimum-norm solution
spends its motion where it is cheapest in activation units. The identity
metric remains available strictly as an ablation. (Estimation detail:
recording $\mathbb{E}[h^2]$ with the controller attached folds the
$e^{2s_j}$ factor in empirically and captures upstream-gate effects on
downstream sites; the closed form (12) is the detached approximation.)
The worked examples show a striking consequence of this metric: it cancels
the activation magnitudes out of the gate kernel entirely, leaving pure
directional information (@sec-linear-metric).

## Conjugate gradients, and why adjoint consistency is non-negotiable

The dual operator $K_\lambda = BM^{-1}B^{\top} + \lambda I$ is accessed only
through matrix–vector products built from two primitives: $v \mapsto Bv$
(forward-mode directional derivative of $F$) and $y \mapsto B^{\top}y$
(reverse-mode gradient of $\langle F, y\rangle$); both primitives are
defined and explained in @sec-app-calculus. Conjugate gradients
(@sec-app-cg) converges to the solution of $K_\lambda \alpha = d$ **only
if** $K_\lambda$ is symmetric positive-definite, and $K_\lambda$ is
symmetric only if the two primitives are exact adjoints of one another —
i.e. computed from the *same* differentiable graph. Substituting a
finite-difference forward product breaks the adjoint identity
$\langle Bv, y\rangle = \langle v, B^{\top}y\rangle$ by an uncontrolled
amount; CG applied to the resulting non-symmetric operator returns numbers,
but they solve no variational problem, and the "residual" computed from
them is not evidence about $\operatorname{range}(B)$. Hence the two
randomized probes reported with every solve:

$$
\text{adjoint gap: }
\frac{\lvert\langle Bv,y\rangle - \langle v,B^{\top}y\rangle\rvert}
     {\lvert\langle Bv,y\rangle\rvert+\lvert\langle v,B^{\top}y\rangle\rvert},
\qquad
\text{symmetry gap: same form on } a^{\top}K_\lambda b \text{ vs } b^{\top}K_\lambda a,
$$

for random unit probes. A single probe is a one-sample check — it certifies
nothing, but it *detects* gross inconsistency with high probability, which
is its job: a gate that must be clean before any residual is read.

## The ridge bias of the reported residual

The reported range residual is computed at $\lambda > 0$, which biases it
*upward*. With the whitened tangent $\tilde B = B M^{-1/2}$ and its SVD
$\tilde B = U\Sigma V^{\top}$ (singular values $\sigma_i$), the solve gives
$B u_\lambda = \sum_i \frac{\sigma_i^2}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}\,
\langle u_i, d\rangle\, u_i$, so

$$
\lVert d - B u_\lambda\rVert^2
= \sum_i \Big(\frac{\lambda}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}\Big)^{\!2}
\langle u_i, d\rangle^2
\;+\; \big\lVert (I - UU^{\top})\, d \big\rVert^2 .
\tag{13}
$$

The second term is the *true* squared distance to the range; the first is
ridge bias, vanishing as $\lambda \to 0$ and negligible whenever $\lambda
\ll \sigma_i^2$ for the components that matter. Consequences worth stating:
the reported residual is an **upper bound** on the true range distance (a
conservative verdict — duality is never over-claimed by ridge bias); and a
suspiciously high residual should be re-run with smaller $\lambda$ and more
CG iterations before being believed, which is the derived content of the
"CG under-solve mimics range failure" failure mode.

# Part V — The residual ladder, term by term {#sec-residuals}

The solve reports a *ladder* of residuals, each isolating one distinct way
the duality story can break. With $u = u_\lambda$ from the dual-form
solution (11):

**Range residual** $\;\lVert d - Bu\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$. The duality
verdict itself: the (ridge-biased, hence conservative) relative distance
from the measured weight-SGD field to the gate tangent's range. High, with
clean probes and converged CG, on a $D \gg k$ observable: the full-weight
step genuinely does something this gate basis cannot express. This number
replaces two retired proxy certificates — a small gate norm and the secant
linearity of @sec-secant — neither of which measures reachability.

**Realized residual**
$\;\lVert d - (F(s+u) - F(s))\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$. The range test
linearizes the chart; this tests the linearization by actually taking the
step. Taylor with remainder: if $B(\cdot)$ is $L_B$-Lipschitz near $s$,

$$
F(s+u) - F(s) \;=\; B(s)\,u + R(u),
\qquad
\lVert R(u)\rVert \le \tfrac12 L_B\,\lVert u\rVert^2 ,
\tag{14}
$$

so realized $\lesssim$ range $+ \tfrac12 L_B\lVert u\rVert^2/\lVert
d\rVert$. A low range residual with a high realized residual therefore
means: the direction exists in the tangent, but the *finite* solved step
left the chart's linear neighborhood — either the target step $\eta$ was too
large (making $\lVert u\rVert$ large) or the chart is too curved here. The
remedy (sweep $\eta$ down) follows from the error term, since
$\lVert u\rVert$ scales linearly with $\lVert d\rVert \propto \eta$ while
the remainder scales quadratically.

**Clipped realized residual.** The deployable update is not $u$ but
$\operatorname{clip}(s+u) - s$, because every applied controller obeys the
box. Re-evaluating the realization after clipping gives the safety-facing
number: what the *bounded* controller actually achieves. Reported beside it:
the fraction of coordinates the clip changed, the fraction left sitting on
the boundary, and $\lVert u - u_{\mathrm{applied}}\rVert / \lVert u\rVert$.
When these are non-trivial, the box — not the geometry — is the binding
constraint, and only the clipped number describes reality.

**The decision order is forced by the derivation.** Probes validate the
operator (else nothing downstream is defined); CG convergence validates the
solve (else the first term of the ridge-bias decomposition (13) is inflated
by an unconverged $\alpha$); range is read next (the claim itself);
realized next (chart curvature, per the Taylor bound (14)); clipping last
(feasibility). Reading them out of order — e.g. quoting a realized residual
when the adjoint probe failed — produces numbers with no referent. This is
the entire content of the documented decision tree, now derived rather than
decreed.

# Part VI — Secant versus pathwise duality {#sec-secant}

## The secant statement and its exact error

An earlier, retired formulation claimed the *trained* controller is
explained by its initialization tangent:
$F(s) - F(0) \approx B(0)\,s$. Call this the **secant statement** — it
compares a finite chord of the chart to the tangent at one endpoint. The
fundamental theorem of calculus gives the chord exactly:

$$
F(s) - F(0) \;=\; \int_0^1 B(t s)\, s \; dt ,
\tag{15}
$$

so the secant error is

$$
F(s) - F(0) - B(0)s \;=\; \int_0^1 \big(B(ts) - B(0)\big)\, s\; dt ,
\tag{16}
$$

— precisely the accumulated *variation of the tangent along the ray*, a
measure of chart curvature between $0$ and $s$, and nothing else. The
diagnostic reports the relative size of the integrated-variation error (16)
at fractions $\alpha \in \{0.125,\dots,1\}$ of the trained gate vector,
plus the cosine between the local tangent direction $B(\alpha s)s$ and the
initial one ("tangent drift").

## Why a failed secant does not refute duality

Duality is a pointwise property of the field and the tangent *at each
state*; the secant is a property of the chart's curvature *along a finite
path*. The two are logically independent, and the chord and error formulas
(15)–(16) make the independence constructive: suppose at every
$t\in[0,1]$ the local solve succeeds exactly, i.e. the local field $d(ts)$
satisfies $d(ts) = B(ts)\,u(ts)$ for bounded $u$. Integrating these local
successes along the path reproduces the *entire* finite displacement as a
sum of locally-dual moves — while the secant error (16) can simultaneously
be arbitrarily large, because it compares against the frozen tangent $B(0)$
rather than the moving one. A trained controller that has wandered into a
curved region of its chart fails the secant *necessarily* and the duality
test *not at all*. The linear worked example computes this error in closed
form (@sec-linear-secant). Hence the standing interpretation rule: the
secant diagnoses the retired global-linearity story; only the range
residual adjudicates duality.

## Pathwise field-locked fitting, stated honestly {#sec-pathwise}

The constructive use of the machinery replaces loss-driven training with
field tracking: repeat { measure the local field $d^{(t)}$ at the current
state $s_t$ (controller attached; weights stepped and restored); solve the
dual system (11) for $u^{(t)}$; set
$s_{t+1} = \operatorname{clip}(s_t + \gamma u^{(t)})$; record the full
residual ladder }. By construction,

$$
F(s_T) - F(s_0) \;=\; \sum_{t} \big(F(s_{t+1}) - F(s_t)\big)
\;\approx\; \sum_t d^{(t)},
$$

with per-step error exactly the recorded realized/clipped residuals. The
honest scope of the emulation: each $d^{(t)}$ is the effect of one weight
step *from the frozen weights, as seen by the currently-gated model* — the
weights never accumulate. The procedure therefore tracks a sequence of
locally-defined SGD fields, not the exact trajectory of a model whose
weights actually move; agreement between the two is itself a first-order
statement, and the per-step residual ledger is what bounds the gap. No
claim beyond that ledger is made.

# Part VII — Worked example 1: one linear block {#sec-worked-linear}

Everything above can be checked by hand on the smallest model that has the
essential structure. This section is deliberately self-contained: every
definition it uses is restated in place, so it can be read (or re-read)
without flipping back to Parts I–VI. Equations local to this example are
numbered (L1), (L2), … to keep them distinct from the global development.

## The model, the gates, and the data

Take a model with **no hidden layers at all**: an input
$x \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$ is multiplied by a weight matrix
$W \in \mathbb{R}^{V\times C}$ to produce logits $z = Wx \in \mathbb{R}^V$
($V$ classes, $C$ input channels). Insert gates on all $C$ input channels:
each channel $j$ carries a signed log-gate $s_j$, and the gated forward
pass is

$$
z(s) \;=\; W\big(e^{s}\odot x\big),
\qquad e^{s}\odot x = (e^{s_1}x_1,\dots,e^{s_C}x_C).
\tag{L1}
$$

At $s=0$ the model is unmodified. Fix two inputs, playing the two roles
defined in Part I:

- a **support input** $x^{S}$ with a class label $y$ — the example the
  imitated SGD step will learn from, via the cross-entropy loss
  $L = -\log p_y$, $p = \operatorname{softmax}(z^S)$;
- a **calibration input** $x^{C}$ — the example on which we watch what any
  intervention does.

As the observable, take *all* $V$ calibration logits: $F(s) = z(s)$
evaluated on $x^C$, so the observable dimension is $D = V$. (Taking all
coordinates rather than a sub-projection keeps the example honest: recall
from Part II that an observable with dimension at most the gate count can
never refute the range condition.)

## The gate tangent, slowly

The gate tangent was defined in Part II as the Jacobian of the observable
with respect to the gate values, $B(s) = \partial F/\partial s$. Compute it
column by column from the gated forward map (L1). Perturbing gate $j$
alone, the only coordinate of the gated input that moves is the $j$-th:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial s_j}\big(e^{s_j} x^{C}_j\big)
= e^{s_j} x^{C}_j ,
$$

and that perturbation enters the logits through column $j$ of $W$.
Therefore

$$
B_{:,j}(s) \;=\; e^{s_j}\, x^{C}_j\; W_{:,j},
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
B(s) = W \,\mathrm{diag}\!\big(e^{s}\odot x^{C}\big).
\tag{L2}
$$

Each tangent column is a *direction in logit space* — the direction the
observable moves when one gate is nudged — and it is the product of three
ingredients: the downstream weight column $W_{:,j}$ (where the channel
points in output space), the activation $x^C_j$ (how strongly the channel
is firing **on the calibration input**), and the current scale $e^{s_j}$.
This is the general tangent-column formula of Part II with the "downstream
Jacobian" specialized to a single matrix.

The data-dependence is stark and worth dwelling on: if $x^{C}_j = 0$, the
entire column is zero. Gate $j$ is then **uncontrollable on this
calibration input** — wiggling it does nothing to the observable —
regardless of how important channel $j$ was on the support input. Keep this
in mind; it is about to matter.

## The weight-SGD step and its effect, slowly

Now compute what one SGD step on $W$ does to the same observable. Three
small steps.

**Step 1: the loss gradient at the support input.** At gate state $s$, the
support logits are $z^S = W(e^s \odot x^S)$, the predicted distribution is
$p = \operatorname{softmax}(z^S)$, and the cross-entropy error signal in
logit space is the familiar

$$
g \;=\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^S} \;=\; p - \mathbf{1}_y
\qquad (\text{probabilities minus one-hot label}).
\tag{L3}
$$

Because $z^S$ is linear in $W$, the weight gradient is the rank-one matrix

$$
\nabla_W L \;=\; g\,\big(e^{s}\odot x^{S}\big)^{\!\top}
\tag{L4}
$$

(each entry: error at output $a$ times gated activation at input $j$).

**Step 2: the step.** SGD with learning rate $\eta$ moves the weights by
$\delta W = -\eta\, g\,(e^s\odot x^S)^{\top}$.

**Step 3: the displacement of the observable.** The calibration logits are
linear in $W$, so the displacement is exact (no higher-order terms in this
example):

$$
d \;=\; \delta W\,\big(e^{s}\odot x^{C}\big)
\;=\; -\,\eta\;
\underbrace{\big\langle e^{s}\odot x^{S},\; e^{s}\odot x^{C}\big\rangle}_{\textstyle \kappa}
\; g .
\tag{L5}
$$

Pause on the structure of this displacement formula. The entire effect of
the weight step on the observable is the support error vector $g$, scaled
by a single number $\kappa$ — the inner product of the two *gated* inputs.
That number **is the empirical neural tangent kernel of this model**: for a
linear model the NTK between two inputs is exactly their inner product (in
the gated coordinates), and the general statement of Part I — "one SGD step
moves the observable by $-\eta$ times the kernel times the error signal" —
collapses to (L5) with the kernel matrix reduced to the scalar $\kappa$.
Two immediate readings:

- If the support and calibration inputs share no active channels,
  $\kappa = 0$: the weight step does nothing visible, and duality holds
  trivially with $u = 0$. The kernel measures *relevance* of the training
  example to the measurement.
- The direction of the displacement is always $g$, no matter what $x^C$
  is. Only its magnitude depends on the calibration input.

## The range condition, exactly

The duality definition of Part III asks: does the measured displacement $d$
lie in the span of the tangent columns? Substituting the tangent (L2) and
the displacement (L5), the question becomes, after dividing out the scalars:

$$
g \;\in\;
\operatorname{span}\big\{\, W_{:,j} \;:\; j \text{ selected},\;
x^{C}_j \neq 0 \,\big\} \;?
\tag{L6}
$$

This is the whole duality question for the linear model, reduced to an
elementary linear-algebra condition, and every regime discussed abstractly
in Parts II–III can now be read off:

- **All gates, generic data, wide input** ($C \ge V$, all channels
  selected, $x^C$ everywhere nonzero, $W$ full rank): the span is all of
  $\mathbb{R}^V$. Duality holds *exactly* — range residual zero — for every
  possible support example. The gate basis is a complete dual description
  of weight-SGD on this observable.
- **Restricted gate budget** ($k < V$ gates selected): the span is a
  $k$-dimensional subspace, and the exact range residual equals the
  relative norm of the component of $g$ orthogonal to the selected columns
  — generically nonzero. Capacity, not cleverness, is the binding
  constraint.
- **Silent calibration channels**: a channel with $x^C_j = 0$ drops out of
  the span even if selected. A weight update may have changed column $j$
  substantially — but notice that the *displacement* (L5) also reaches the
  observable only through the active channels (the $j$-th term of $\kappa$
  vanishes), so what the weight step "did" to silent channels was equally
  invisible. Controllability and visibility fail together. This alignment
  becomes a theorem in the MLP example.
- **Narrow observable**: if instead of all $V$ logits we observed only the
  teacher coordinate ($D = 1$), condition (L6) collapses to "some selected
  $j$ has $W_{yj}\,x^C_j \neq 0$" — essentially always true. The narrow
  observable cannot refute; this is the dimension-counting warning of
  Part II made concrete.

## A numeric miniature

Small enough to verify mentally. Let $V = C = 2$,

$$
W = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
x^{C} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
x^{S} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
y = 2,
\qquad
s = 0 .
$$

Tangent columns from (L2): $B_{:,1} = x^C_1 W_{:,1} = (1,0)^{\top}$ and
$B_{:,2} = x^C_2 W_{:,2} = \mathbf{0}$ (channel 2 is silent on the
calibration input). So $\operatorname{range}(B) =
\operatorname{span}\{(1,0)^{\top}\}$ — a line.

Support side: $z^S = Wx^S = (2,1)^{\top}$,
$p = \operatorname{softmax}(2,1) \approx (0.731, 0.269)$, and the error
signal (L3) is $g = p - (0,1)^{\top} \approx (0.731, -0.731)^{\top}$. The
kernel scalar is $\kappa = \langle x^S, x^C\rangle = 1$, so the
displacement (L5) is $d \approx -\eta\,(0.731, -0.731)^{\top}$.

Project $d$ onto the line: the reachable part is its first coordinate; the
unreachable part is $(0, 0.731\eta)^{\top}$. Exact range residual:

$$
\frac{\lVert d - Bu^\star\rVert}{\lVert d\rVert}
= \frac{0.731\,\eta}{0.731\,\eta\,\sqrt2}
= \frac{1}{\sqrt2} \approx 0.707 .
$$

A 71% range residual: most of what this SGD step does is invisible to the
gates — and the diagnosis is legible. The step wants to *lower* logit 1 and
*raise* logit 2 (push probability toward the label $y=2$). Raising logit 2
requires moving along $W_{:,2} = (1,1)^{\top}$, i.e. through channel 2 —
the one channel the calibration input never activates. The gate that could
have helped is uncontrollable *on this data*. Duality failure here is not
a defect of the solver; it is a true statement about what this gate basis
can express on this input.

## The activation metric, and a small surprise {#sec-linear-metric}

Part IV argued that gate motion should be measured in the metric pulled
back from activation space: $M_j = (\text{activation energy of channel }
j)\times e^{2s_j}$, here simply $M_j = e^{2s_j}(x^C_j)^2$. Form the gate
kernel $B M^{-1} B^{\top}$ that the dual solve inverts, using the tangent
(L2):

$$
B M^{-1} B^{\top}
= \sum_{j\ \mathrm{selected},\ x^C_j \neq 0}
\frac{\big(e^{s_j}x^C_j\big)^2}{e^{2s_j}(x^C_j)^2}\; W_{:,j}W_{:,j}^{\top}
= \sum_{j\ \mathrm{active}} W_{:,j}\,W_{:,j}^{\top} .
\tag{L7}
$$

Every activation magnitude and every current gate value cancels. Under the
pullback metric, the gate kernel is the bare sum of outer products of the
downstream weight columns over controllable channels — *pure directional
information about where the channels point in output space*, with the
data's loudness normalized away. That is exactly what a well-chosen metric
is for: it makes the solve answer "is the direction expressible?" rather
than "is the direction expressible by loud channels?".

## Curvature: the realized residual at second order

The range condition tested the *linearized* chart. The realized residual of
Part V tests the linearization by actually taking the solved step $u$. In
this example the chart's curvature is fully explicit, because $z$ is linear
in $e^s$ but exponential in $s$. Expand the true step using
$e^{u_j} = 1 + u_j + u_j^2/2 + \cdots$:

$$
F(s+u) - F(s)
= W\big(e^{s}\odot(e^{u}-1)\odot x^{C}\big)
= \underbrace{B(s)\,u}_{\text{linear prediction}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac12\, W\big(e^{s}\odot u^{2}\odot x^{C}\big)}_{\text{curvature}}
\;+\; O(u^3).
\tag{L8}
$$

The deviation between "what the tangent predicted" and "what the step did"
is quadratic in the update, channel-wise, amplified through $W$ — the
concrete instance of the Taylor-remainder bound in Part V. With the
reference box $s_{\max} = 0.05$, every $|u_j| \le 0.1$, so the relative
curvature term is at most a few percent: the analytic reason small gate
boxes keep realized residuals close to range residuals, and the analytic
reason a *large* solved update (from too large a target step $\eta$)
inflates the realized residual quadratically while the range residual stays
put.

## The secant error in closed form {#sec-linear-secant}

Part VI claimed the secant diagnostic measures chart curvature along the
training ray and nothing else. Here that is a two-line computation. Along
the ray $\alpha s$ (scaling a trained gate vector $s$ from 0 to 1), the
chord is $F(\alpha s) - F(0) = W\big((e^{\alpha s}-1)\odot x^C\big)$ and
the initial-tangent prediction is $B(0)\,\alpha s = W\big(\alpha s\odot
x^C\big)$. Their difference is

$$
\text{secant error}(\alpha)
= W\Big(\big(e^{\alpha s} - 1 - \alpha s\big)\odot x^{C}\Big),
\tag{L9}
$$

with the per-channel scalar deviation $e^{\xi}-1-\xi \ge 0$ growing
quadratically in $\xi = \alpha s_j$. Meanwhile, at *every* point $\alpha s$
along the ray, the local tangent is $B(\alpha s) = W\,\mathrm{diag}
(e^{\alpha s}\odot x^C)$ — same column space as $B(0)$ wherever
$x^C_j \neq 0$ — so every local duality solve that succeeded at $s=0$
succeeds identically along the whole ray. The secant error (L9) grows;
duality never budges. This is the promised worked instance of "secant
failure is not dual failure": the two diagnostics measure different things,
visibly.

## What this example teaches

- The tangent column = (downstream direction) × (calibration activation) ×
  (current scale); silent channels are uncontrollable. — from (L2)
- The NTK is an alignment score; for a linear model it is literally the
  inner product of the gated inputs, and it scales — never redirects — the
  displacement. — from (L5)
- Duality is an elementary span condition, with exact success and failure
  regimes readable from the data and the gate budget. — from (L6)
- The activation metric cancels loudness from the gate kernel, leaving
  directions. — from (L7)
- Realized-vs-range gaps are quadratic curvature, controlled by the box. —
  from (L8)
- The secant error is exponential-vs-linear gap accumulation, orthogonal to
  duality. — from (L9)

# Part VII′ — Worked example 2: a two-layer MLP {#sec-worked-mlp}

The linear example has one weight matrix and no depth, so two essential
phenomena are missing: a weight update *upstream* of the gate site, and a
nonlinearity between layers. The smallest model with both is a two-layer
MLP. This section is again self-contained, with local equation numbers
(M1), (M2), …; it culminates in a structural result the linear model cannot
exhibit — gates absorb upstream weight updates *exactly*, channel by
channel, and downstream updates only through a span condition.

## The model and the gate site

Input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{C_0}$; first layer $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{H\times
C_0}$ produces pre-activations $a = W_1 x$; an elementwise nonlinearity
$\phi$ produces hidden activations $h = \phi(a) \in \mathbb{R}^H$; the
gates sit **on the hidden layer** (the analogue of a "block output" site):
$\tilde h = e^{s}\odot h$ with $s \in \mathbb{R}^H$; the second layer
$W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{V\times H}$ produces logits:

$$
z(s) \;=\; W_2\big(e^{s}\odot \phi(W_1 x)\big).
\tag{M1}
$$

Take $\phi = \mathrm{ReLU}$ as the running choice (the role of this choice
is examined below). As before: a support input $x^S$ with label $y$ and
cross-entropy loss, a calibration input $x^C$, the observable
$F(s) = z(s)$ at $x^C$ with $D = V$. Notation for the two passes:

$$
a^{S} = W_1 x^{S},\quad h^{S} = \phi(a^{S}),
\qquad
a^{C} = W_1 x^{C},\quad h^{C} = \phi(a^{C}).
$$

## The gate tangent

Identical reasoning to the linear case: perturbing $s_j$ perturbs the gated
hidden coordinate by $e^{s_j} h^{C}_j\,\delta s_j$, which reaches the
logits through column $j$ of $W_2$:

$$
B_{:,j}(s) \;=\; e^{s_j}\, h^{C}_j\; (W_2)_{:,j},
\qquad
B(s) = W_2\,\mathrm{diag}\!\big(e^{s}\odot h^{C}\big).
\tag{M2}
$$

Same three-factor structure — downstream column, calibration activation,
current scale — except the "activation" is now a computed hidden feature
$h^C_j = \phi(a^C_j)$ rather than a raw input. A hidden channel that the
calibration input does not excite (for ReLU: $a^C_j \le 0$, so
$h^C_j = 0$) is uncontrollable, exactly as before.

## The weight-SGD step: now two matrices

The step touches *both* layers, and this is where the example earns its
keep. Compute the two gradients by ordinary backpropagation through the
gated forward map (M1) at the support input.

**Output layer.** The error signal in logit space is again
$g = \operatorname{softmax}(z^S) - \mathbf{1}_y$, and since
$z^S = W_2 \tilde h^S$ is linear in $W_2$:

$$
\nabla_{W_2} L \;=\; g\,\big(e^{s}\odot h^{S}\big)^{\!\top}.
\tag{M3}
$$

**Hidden layer.** Backpropagate $g$ through $W_2$, the gates, and the
nonlinearity:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \tilde h^S} = W_2^{\top} g
\;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h^S} = e^{s}\odot\big(W_2^{\top} g\big)
\;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
\delta \;\equiv\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^S}
= e^{s}\odot\big(W_2^{\top} g\big)\odot \phi'(a^{S}),
$$

and since $a^S = W_1 x^S$ is linear in $W_1$:

$$
\nabla_{W_1} L \;=\; \delta\,(x^{S})^{\top}.
\tag{M4}
$$

The SGD step is $\delta W_2 = -\eta\,\nabla_{W_2}L$ and
$\delta W_1 = -\eta\,\nabla_{W_1}L$ simultaneously.

## The displacement, layer by layer

To first order, the observable's displacement is the sum of the two
layers' contributions (the Jacobian with respect to all weights is the
concatenation of per-layer Jacobians, so the kernel of Part I decomposes
into a per-layer sum — a fully general fact, visible here concretely).

**Contribution of the $W_2$ step.** The calibration logits are linear in
$W_2$, so

$$
d^{(2)} = \delta W_2\,\big(e^{s}\odot h^{C}\big)
= -\,\eta\;\kappa_2\; g,
\qquad
\kappa_2 = \big\langle e^{s}\odot h^{S},\, e^{s}\odot h^{C}\big\rangle
= \sum_j e^{2s_j} h^{S}_j h^{C}_j .
\tag{M5}
$$

Structurally identical to the linear example's displacement: the error
vector $g$, scaled by a kernel — now the inner product of the *gated
hidden* representations. $\kappa_2$ is the output layer's contribution to
the empirical NTK.

**Contribution of the $W_1$ step.** Push the perturbation forward through
the calibration pass: $\delta a^C = \delta W_1 x^C = -\eta\,\delta\,
\langle x^S, x^C\rangle$, then through the nonlinearity
($\delta h^C = \phi'(a^C)\odot\delta a^C$), the gates, and $W_2$:

$$
d^{(1)}
= -\,\eta\;\kappa_1\;
W_2\Big( e^{2s}\odot \phi'(a^{S})\odot \phi'(a^{C})\odot
\big(W_2^{\top} g\big)\Big),
\qquad
\kappa_1 = \big\langle x^{S}, x^{C}\big\rangle .
\tag{M6}
$$

Define the diagonal matrix
$\Delta = \mathrm{diag}\big(e^{2s_j}\,\phi'(a^S_j)\,\phi'(a^C_j)\big)$ to
compress the channel-wise factor; then the total measured field is

$$
d \;=\; d^{(2)} + d^{(1)}
\;=\; -\,\eta\Big(\kappa_2\, g
\;+\; \kappa_1\, W_2\,\Delta\, W_2^{\top}\, g\Big).
\tag{M7}
$$

Read the two-layer NTK off this decomposition: the output layer contributes
a scalar kernel $\kappa_2$ acting directly on $g$; the hidden layer
contributes an *operator* kernel $\kappa_1 W_2 \Delta W_2^{\top}$ — the
input-space alignment $\kappa_1$, sandwiched by the downstream matrix, with
each channel switched on only if it is active (nonzero $\phi'$) on **both**
the support and the calibration pass. Depth turned the kernel from a number
into a structured operator, but every factor remains legible.

## The structural result: upstream is exact, downstream is a span

Now ask the duality question — can a gate update $u$ reproduce $d$? — by
matching against the tangent columns (M2). Treat the two contributions
separately.

**The upstream ($W_1$) contribution is realizable exactly, channel by
channel.** Expand the hidden-layer displacement (M6) over the columns of
$W_2$:

$$
d^{(1)} = \sum_{j} (W_2)_{:,j}\,
\Big[-\eta\,\kappa_1\, e^{2s_j}\phi'(a^S_j)\phi'(a^C_j)\,
\big(W_2^{\top}g\big)_j\Big],
$$

a weighted sum of *exactly the same columns* that span the gate tangent.
Matching coefficients with $Bu = \sum_j u_j\,e^{s_j}h^C_j\,(W_2)_{:,j}$
gives a closed-form gate update realizing $d^{(1)}$ perfectly:

$$
u^{(1)}_j \;=\;
-\,\eta\,\kappa_1\;
\frac{e^{s_j}\,\phi'(a^{S}_j)\,\phi'(a^{C}_j)\,\big(W_2^{\top}g\big)_j}
     {h^{C}_j}
\qquad\text{for every } j \text{ with } h^{C}_j \neq 0 .
\tag{M8}
$$

What about channels with $h^C_j = 0$, where the update formula divides by
zero? For ReLU they never arise: $h^C_j = 0$ means $a^C_j \le 0$, hence
$\phi'(a^C_j) = 0$, hence the $j$-th term of $d^{(1)}$ was *absent to begin
with*. The channels the upstream weight step visibly moves and the channels
the gates control are **the same channels** — visibility and
controllability coincide, structurally, because both are gated by the same
activation pattern. (Honest caveat: this coincidence is a property of
ReLU-like activations, where zero output implies zero slope. For an odd
saturating activation such as $\tanh$, a channel can sit exactly at
$h^C_j = \phi(0) = 0$ with slope $\phi'(0) = 1$ — visible to the weight
step yet invisible to a multiplicative gate. For generic inputs this is a
measure-zero coincidence, but it marks a real boundary of the mechanism:
multiplicative gates cannot act on channels with nothing to multiply.)

**The downstream ($W_2$) contribution is realizable only up to a span
condition.** The output-layer displacement (M5) is the vector
$-\eta\kappa_2\,g$, and $g$ is whatever the support example made it — there
is no reason for it to lie in
$\operatorname{span}\{(W_2)_{:,j} : j\ \text{selected},\ h^C_j \neq 0\}$.
If the active selected columns span $\mathbb{R}^V$ (guaranteed when
$H \ge V$, $W_2$ full rank, all calibration-active channels selected), the
contribution is realizable exactly and total duality holds with residual
zero. Otherwise the range residual equals the relative norm of the
component of $\kappa_2\,g$ outside the span — the same failure geometry as
the linear example's condition (L6), now attached specifically to the
*downstream* layer.

> **Summary of the structural result.** A gate site absorbs the SGD update
> of the weight matrix **immediately upstream of it** exactly and channel
> by channel, with the explicit update (M8) — because an upstream weight
> step and a gate step act on the observable through the *same* bottleneck,
> the site's channels. The update of the matrix **downstream** of the site
> is absorbed only insofar as its effect happens to lie in the span of what
> the site feeds into. Upstream: per-channel identity. Downstream: span
> condition.

This result is the first-principles justification for a design choice that
otherwise looks like engineering convention: **gate every block**. In a
deep network with a gate site at every block's output, every weight matrix
has a gated site immediately downstream of it, so every layer's
contribution to the SGD field falls under the favorable, exactly-absorbable
case — *up to* the sparsity of the per-site gate budget (only the top-$k$
saliency-selected channels are gated) and the cross-terms of depth. The
range residual measures precisely the price of those two compromises. It
also illuminates the two hook-site variants: gating a block's *input*
versus its *output* shifts which weight matrices count as "immediately
upstream," which is why theorem-facing claims are asked to ablate the hook
site.

## Where curvature comes from in deep gated models

In both examples so far, the tangent's dependence on $s$ was the explicit
factor $e^{s_j}$ only, because there was a single gated site and nothing
upstream of it moved. Add a second gated site and genuine *cross-curvature*
appears. Gate the input as well: $s^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_0}$ on $x$ and
$s^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ on $h$, so
$z = W_2\big(e^{s^{(1)}}\odot\phi\big(W_1(e^{s^{(0)}}\odot x)\big)\big)$.
The tangent column of an input gate $i$ is now

$$
\frac{\partial z}{\partial s^{(0)}_i}
= W_2\Big(e^{s^{(1)}}\odot \phi'\!\big(a(s^{(0)})\big)\odot
(W_1)_{:,i}\, e^{s^{(0)}_i} x_i\Big),
\qquad a(s^{(0)}) = W_1\big(e^{s^{(0)}}\odot x\big),
\tag{M9}
$$

which depends on $s^{(1)}$ (explicitly) *and* on $s^{(0)}$ (through the
pre-activations inside $\phi'$). The mixed second derivative
$\partial^2 z/\partial s^{(1)}_j \partial s^{(0)}_i$ is generically
nonzero: moving a downstream gate changes what an upstream gate's nudge
does. This is the precise content of the claim in Part II that "the chart
is curved and its tangent must be re-evaluated wherever it is used," and it
is also why the activation metric is best *recorded with the controller
attached* (the hidden energies $\mathbb{E}[h^2]$ at a site change when
upstream gates move) rather than computed from the detached closed form.

## What the MLP example adds to the linear one

- The empirical NTK decomposes over layers; each layer contributes a kernel
  whose factors (input alignment, downstream sandwich, joint activation
  gating) are individually meaningful. — from the layer-by-layer field
  (M5)–(M7)
- Gates absorb the upstream layer's SGD step exactly, with a closed-form
  update; visibility and controllability coincide channel-wise under
  ReLU-like activations. — from the exact-absorption formula (M8)
- Downstream layers reduce to the span condition already seen in the
  linear example; total duality splits cleanly into "free" upstream parts
  and "span-priced" downstream parts.
- Gating every block is not a convention but the configuration that puts
  every weight matrix into the exactly-absorbable position.
- Multiple gated sites introduce gate–gate cross-curvature — the mechanism
  behind realized-vs-range gaps and the state-dependent metric in deep
  models. — from the two-site tangent (M9)

## What full depth adds beyond the MLP

In a transformer-scale network, the tangent column (M2) gains a long
downstream Jacobian (attention mixes token positions, so a gate at one
position influences logits at others); the per-layer kernels of the
decomposition (M7) sum over dozens of blocks and all their parameter
matrices; the activation pattern factor $\Delta$ becomes a soft,
data-dependent modulation rather than a hard ReLU mask; and the
cross-curvature (M9) compounds across every pair of gated sites. None of
the *logic* changes — only the feasibility of closed forms, which is
precisely why the deep-network instrument is the measured, probe-guarded
solver of Part IV rather than a formula.

# Part VIII — Reading real diagnostics {#sec-reading}

A compressed translation table from numbers to conclusions, each row now
justified by a derivation above:

| Observation | Conclusion | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| adjoint or symmetry probe large | no conclusion possible; operator invalid | @sec-solver (CG requires SPD) |
| CG not converged | residual inflated; raise iterations, lower $\lambda$ | ridge-bias decomposition (13) |
| range residual low on **target** only | weak evidence; observable too small to refute | @sec-observables dimension count; linear example's narrow-observable case in @sec-worked-linear |
| range low, realized high | direction reachable, step too big / chart curved; sweep $\eta$ | Taylor bound (14); explicit curvature (L8) |
| realized low, clipped high, clip fraction high | geometry fine; the *box* is the obstruction; report clipped numbers | @sec-residuals |
| secant error high at $\alpha=1$ | chart curved along training path; says nothing about duality | chord/error formulas (15)–(16); closed form (L9) |
| gate norm small | no duality evidence whatsoever | norm measures motion in the chart, not reachability of $d$ |
| range residual high, all gates clean, $D \gg k$ | genuine refutation: weight-SGD's effect is not expressible by this gate basis here | @sec-duality; span conditions (L6), (M5)-vs-span |

And the standing scientific posture, restated once: the duality is a
*hypothesis with an instrument*, not an assumption of the method. The
deployable training path never relies on it; the diagnostic path can refute
it on any given model, data, observable, and gate budget; and every
quantity that could silently corrupt the verdict (adjointness, convergence,
ridge bias, curvature, clipping) is measured and reported alongside.

# Limitations and open questions {#sec-limits}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Critical commentary
A companion document, [*Gating as Fine-Tuning on an Orbit*
](gating_equivalence_notes.qmd), develops the limitations below into a
full critical examination: the exact orbit characterization, a simplified
three-rung proof, the theorem-vs-test distinction, duality as a
distribution over batches, prior-art positioning, and remedies when the
residual is large.
:::

- **Locality.** Everything is first-order at a state. Accumulated duality
  along long paths is controlled only by the per-step residual ledger; no
  uniform-in-time statement is made or tested.
- **Observable-relativity.** Duality on one projection does not imply
  duality on a larger one (and @sec-observables shows small projections can
  never refute). Claims should name their observable.
- **One step, one batch.** The target is a single SGD step on one support
  batch. Duality against an *averaged* field (full-batch gradient flow), or
  against optimizers with state (momentum, Adam), is a strictly stronger
  claim, not tested by this instrument.
- **Selection coupling.** The gate basis was chosen by saliency on the same
  loss whose SGD field it is later asked to span; the degree to which
  selection *causes* duality (versus capacity alone) is exactly what the
  random-gate control measures.
- **Activation-pattern dependence.** The MLP example shows both the field
  and the tangent are gated by which channels fire on which batch;
  controllability is a property of (model, data) jointly, not of the model
  alone. Duality verdicts should be expected to vary across data domains.
- **Cost.** The target measurement differentiates model weights; the honest
  mitigations (parameter filters, small batches, modest observables) trade
  coverage for feasibility and should be disclosed with any claim.

# Appendix A — The calculus toolkit {.appendix #sec-app-calculus}

*Self-contained prerequisites: Jacobians, directional derivatives, JVP/VJP,
Taylor remainders.*

**Jacobian.** For a differentiable map $F:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the
Jacobian at $x$ is the $m\times n$ matrix $J(x)$ of partial derivatives,
$J_{ab} = \partial F_a/\partial x_b$. It is the unique linear map with

$$
F(x + v) \;=\; F(x) + J(x)\,v + o(\lVert v\rVert).
$$

**Directional derivative / JVP.** The product $J(x)\,v$ — the first-order
response of $F$ to moving along $v$ — can be computed *without forming
$J$*: it is the derivative of the scalar-parameter curve
$t \mapsto F(x + tv)$ at $t=0$. Automatic differentiation systems compute
it directly ("forward mode"); the operation is called a
**Jacobian–vector product (JVP)**. Cost: about one extra forward pass per
direction.

**Gradient / VJP.** The product $J(x)^{\top} y$ — for $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$
— is the gradient of the scalar function $x \mapsto \langle F(x),
y\rangle$. Reverse-mode automatic differentiation (backpropagation)
computes it in about one backward pass; the operation is called a
**vector–Jacobian product (VJP)**. The two primitives are linked by the
**adjoint identity**, which is just associativity of the inner product:

$$
\langle J v,\, y\rangle \;=\; \langle v,\, J^{\top} y\rangle
\qquad \text{for all } v, y .
$$

When the JVP and the VJP are computed from the same differentiable program,
this identity holds to floating-point precision; if either side is
approximated differently (e.g. JVP by finite differences), it fails by an
uncontrolled amount — the root cause of the adjoint-consistency requirement
in Part IV.

**Taylor with remainder.** If $F$ is twice differentiable with second
derivative bounded by $L$ near $x$:

$$
\big\lVert F(x+v) - F(x) - J(x)\,v \big\rVert \;\le\; \tfrac{L}{2}\,
\lVert v\rVert^2 .
$$

This single inequality powers three separate arguments in the main text:
the validity of the one-step NTK displacement (Part I), the
finite-difference step-size trade-off (Part I), and the realized-residual
error bound (Part V).

**Chain rule, compositional form.** For $F = F_2 \circ F_1$, the Jacobian
is $J = J_2 J_1$. Backpropagation is the observation that
$J^{\top} y = J_1^{\top}(J_2^{\top} y)$ can be evaluated right-to-left with
only VJPs. Every gradient in the worked examples (e.g. the backprop chain
for the hidden-layer gradient (M4)) is this rule applied twice or three
times.

# Appendix B — The neural tangent kernel in one page {.appendix #sec-app-ntk}

*Self-contained prerequisite: what the NTK is and why it appears.*

Let $f(\theta, x)$ be a network output (a scalar coordinate, for
simplicity) and train $\theta$ by gradient descent on a loss
$L(\theta) = \sum_i \ell\big(f(\theta, x_i)\big)$ over examples $x_i$.
Consider continuous-time gradient descent ("gradient flow"):
$\dot\theta = -\nabla_\theta L$. How does the output at an arbitrary probe
point $x'$ evolve? Chain rule:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} f(\theta, x')
= \big\langle \nabla_\theta f(\theta, x'),\ \dot\theta\big\rangle
= -\sum_i
\underbrace{\big\langle \nabla_\theta f(\theta,x'),\
\nabla_\theta f(\theta,x_i)\big\rangle}_{\textstyle K(x', x_i)}
\;\ell'\big(f(\theta,x_i)\big).
$$

The training dynamics of the *outputs* close on themselves up to the
kernel

$$
K(x', x) \;=\; \big\langle \nabla_\theta f(\theta, x'),\,
\nabla_\theta f(\theta, x)\big\rangle ,
$$

the **(empirical) neural tangent kernel**: the alignment of the two
points' weight-sensitivities. Outputs at probe points strongly aligned
with a training point move a lot when that point is trained on; orthogonal
ones do not move at all. In matrix form for vector outputs, $K = J' J^\top$
with $J$ the Jacobian of outputs with respect to weights — the form used in
Part I, where one *discrete* SGD step gives the displacement
$\delta f \approx -\eta\, K\, \ell'$ at first order.

Two layers of theory sit above this identity, neither needed in this
report but both worth naming. (i) *Infinite-width*: Jacot, Gabriel &
Hongler (2018) showed that for standard initializations, as layer widths
grow, $K$ at initialization converges to a deterministic kernel and — more
surprisingly — stays constant during training, so the network trains
exactly like kernel regression with that fixed kernel. (ii) *Lazy
training*: Chizat, Oyallon & Bach (2019) characterized when finite networks
operate in this linearized regime (weights barely move relative to their
scale). The duality test of this report uses only the *finite-width,
single-step, measured* version: no constancy, no width limit — the
Jacobians are whatever the frozen network's are, the step is actually
taken, and the displacement is actually measured.

# Appendix C — Mirror descent and exponentiated gradients {.appendix #sec-app-mirror}

*Self-contained prerequisite: why log coordinates are the natural home of
multiplicative parameters.*

Plain gradient descent $w \leftarrow w - \eta\nabla L$ implicitly treats
parameter space as Euclidean: it solves, at each step,

$$
w_{t+1} = \arg\min_w\
\Big\{ \langle \nabla L(w_t),\, w\rangle
+ \tfrac{1}{2\eta}\lVert w - w_t\rVert_2^2 \Big\}
$$

— "move along the gradient, but stay close to where you are, *in the
Euclidean metric*." **Mirror descent** (Nemirovski & Yudin 1983; Beck &
Teboulle 2003) generalizes the closeness term: pick a strictly convex
*mirror map* $\Psi$, measure closeness by its Bregman divergence
$D_\Psi(w, w_t) = \Psi(w)-\Psi(w_t)-\langle\nabla\Psi(w_t), w-w_t\rangle$,
and solve the same proximal problem. The optimality condition gives the
clean two-step description:

$$
\nabla\Psi(w_{t+1}) \;=\; \nabla\Psi(w_t) \;-\; \eta\,\nabla L(w_t)
$$

— *map to the dual ("mirror") coordinates $\nabla\Psi(w)$, take an
ordinary additive gradient step there, map back.*

**The case that matters here.** For positive parameters $m \in
\mathbb{R}_{>0}^k$ take the (unnormalized) negative entropy
$\Psi(m) = \sum_j (m_j\log m_j - m_j)$. Then $\nabla\Psi(m) = \log m$: the
mirror coordinate is the **logarithm**, and the mirror-descent update is

$$
\log m_{t+1} = \log m_t - \eta\,\nabla L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
m_{t+1} = m_t \odot e^{-\eta \nabla L},
$$

the classical **exponentiated-gradient / multiplicative-weights** update
(Kivinen & Warmuth 1997). Additive in log space *is* multiplicative in the
original space; positivity is preserved automatically; and composing two
updates multiplies the scales, i.e. adds the logs.

**Connection to the gates.** A signed log-gate $s_j$ is exactly the mirror
coordinate of the channel scale $m_j = e^{s_j}$. Three method-level facts
are restatements of this: training the gates by gradient descent *in $s$*
is exponentiated-gradient on the channel scales; controller composition
("add the $s$ vectors") is linearity in mirror coordinates; and the box
$|s_j| \le s_{\max}$ is a trust region that is symmetric in the mirror
space — equal multiplicative headroom up ($\times e^{s_{\max}}$) and down
($\times e^{-s_{\max}}$), which a box on $m$ itself would not give.

# Appendix D — Least squares, ridge, minimum norm, and the dual system {.appendix #sec-app-ls}

*Self-contained prerequisite: the linear-algebra of the solver.*

**Least squares.** Given $B \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times k}$ and $d \in
\mathbb{R}^D$, the least-squares problem $\min_u \lVert Bu - d\rVert^2$
has optimality ("normal equations") condition $B^{\top}B\,u = B^{\top}d$.
The minimum value is the squared distance from $d$ to
$\operatorname{range}(B)$ — which is why a least-squares residual answers
a *range* question.

**Minimum-norm solutions and Lagrange multipliers.** When $Bu = d$ has many
solutions (typical when $k > D$), the canonical choice is the smallest one
in a chosen inner product, $\min \tfrac12 u^{\top}Mu$ subject to $Bu = d$
with $M \succ 0$. Introduce a multiplier $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^D$ for the
constraint; stationarity of the Lagrangian
$\tfrac12 u^{\top}Mu - \alpha^{\top}(Bu - d)$ in $u$ gives
$u = M^{-1}B^{\top}\alpha$, and feasibility gives the **dual system**
$\big(BM^{-1}B^{\top}\big)\alpha = d$ — a $D\times D$ problem whose unknown
is the multiplier. The solution $u$ is a combination of the rows of $B$
(reweighted by $M^{-1}$): minimum-norm solutions live in the row space.

**Ridge (Tikhonov) regularization.** Both problems are unified and
stabilized by $\min_u \lVert Bu-d\rVert^2 + \lambda\lVert u\rVert_M^2$,
$\lambda>0$, whose solution can be written in primal or dual form,

$$
u_\lambda = \big(B^{\top}B + \lambda M\big)^{-1} B^{\top} d
\;=\; M^{-1}B^{\top}\big(BM^{-1}B^{\top} + \lambda I\big)^{-1} d ,
$$

the equality being the **push-through identity**, proved in one line by
multiplying out $B^{\top}\big(BM^{-1}B^{\top} + \lambda I\big) =
\big(B^{\top}B + \lambda M\big)M^{-1}B^{\top}$. As $\lambda\to0^+$,
$u_\lambda$ tends to the minimum-$M$-norm least-squares solution.

**Spectral picture.** Whiten: $\tilde B = BM^{-1/2}$ with SVD $\tilde B =
U\Sigma V^{\top}$. The ridge solution filters each singular direction by
the factor $\sigma_i^2/(\sigma_i^2+\lambda)$: directions with
$\sigma_i^2 \gg \lambda$ pass, those with $\sigma_i^2 \ll \lambda$ are
suppressed. The residual decomposes into a vanishing-with-$\lambda$ bias
on $\operatorname{range}(B)$ plus the true out-of-range component — the
decomposition used in Part IV to show the reported residual is a
conservative upper bound.

# Appendix E — Conjugate gradients, matrix-free {.appendix #sec-app-cg}

*Self-contained prerequisite: the iterative solver and its contract.*

**Problem class.** CG solves $A\alpha = d$ for **symmetric
positive-definite** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times D}$, using only
matrix–vector products $y \mapsto Ay$ — never $A$ itself. This is what
makes it usable when $A = BM^{-1}B^{\top} + \lambda I$ exists only as a
composition of JVP and VJP calls through a network.

**Idea.** CG minimizes the quadratic
$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac12\alpha^{\top}A\alpha - d^{\top}\alpha$ (whose
unique minimizer is the solution) over a growing family of Krylov
subspaces $\mathrm{span}\{d, Ad, A^2d, \dots\}$, choosing search directions
that are *$A$-conjugate* (orthogonal in the $A$-inner product) so that each
one-dimensional minimization never spoils the previous ones. In exact
arithmetic it terminates in at most $D$ steps; in practice it converges
fast when the eigenvalues of $A$ are clustered, and the ridge term
$+\lambda I$ bounds the spectrum away from zero, which both guarantees
positive-definiteness and helps conditioning.

**The contract, and what breaks it.** Everything above presumes $A =
A^{\top} \succ 0$: conjugacy, the minimization interpretation, and the
convergence theory all use symmetry. Feed CG a non-symmetric operator and
the iteration still produces vectors, but they minimize nothing and solve
nothing in general — there is no meaningful "residual." The operator built
from an exact JVP/VJP pair is symmetric by the adjoint identity of
@sec-app-calculus; an operator mixing a finite-difference forward product
with an autograd transpose is not. This is the entire justification for
the adjoint and symmetry probes of Part IV: they test the contract under
which CG's output means anything.

**Practical readouts.** The residual history $\lVert d -
A\alpha_t\rVert$ should decrease (not necessarily monotonically in the
plain residual, but trending); a solve stopped before the tolerance leaves
the computed range residual *too large*, never too small — consistent with
the conservative-verdict property of Part IV. The honest order of checks:
probes first, convergence flag second, residuals last.

# References {#sec-refs .unnumbered}

- Jacot, A., Gabriel, F., Hongler, C. (2018). *Neural Tangent Kernel:
  Convergence and Generalization in Neural Networks.* NeurIPS.
- Lee, J., Xiao, L., Schoenholz, S., Bahri, Y., Novak, R., Sohl-Dickstein,
  J., Pennington, J. (2019). *Wide Neural Networks of Any Depth Evolve as
  Linear Models Under Gradient Descent.* NeurIPS.
- Chizat, L., Oyallon, E., Bach, F. (2019). *On Lazy Training in
  Differentiable Programming.* NeurIPS.
- Nemirovski, A., Yudin, D. (1983). *Problem Complexity and Method
  Efficiency in Optimization.* Wiley.
- Beck, A., Teboulle, M. (2003). *Mirror Descent and Nonlinear Projected
  Subgradient Methods for Convex Optimization.* Operations Research
  Letters 31(3).
- Kivinen, J., Warmuth, M. (1997). *Exponentiated Gradient versus Gradient
  Descent for Linear Predictors.* Information and Computation 132(1).
- Hestenes, M., Stiefel, E. (1952). *Methods of Conjugate Gradients for
  Solving Linear Systems.* Journal of Research of the National Bureau of
  Standards 49(6).
